title: Nvidia hand-delivers first Vera CPUs to Anthropic, OpenAI, SpaceX, and Oracle
subtitle: The Grace successor uses Nvidia's own Olympus cores and is pitched as the first CPU purpose-built for agentic AI workloads.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Nvidia's Ian Buck hand-delivered the first production Vera CPUs to Anthropic in San Francisco, OpenAI in Mission Bay, and SpaceX in Palo Alto on Friday, followed by Oracle Cloud Infrastructure in Santa Clara on Monday. CEO Jensen Huang confirmed the customer list during his Computex keynote in Taiwan last week. Oracle is set to be the first hyperscaler to deploy Vera at scale, with broader availability on the other major clouds expected in the second half of 2026. Full production starts in Q3.

Vera is the successor to Grace, Nvidia's previous data-center CPU. The architectural change is the part worth tracking: Vera ships with 88 of Nvidia's in-house Olympus cores, replacing the off-the-shelf Arm Neoverse cores Grace used. That brings the host CPU under the same roof as Nvidia's GPU and networking designs, and removes a piece of the data-center stack that Nvidia previously rented from Arm. The marketing line is that Vera is the first CPU purpose-built for the agentic AI era — meaning workloads where the host processor is no longer just feeding tensors to GPUs but is also running the orchestration logic, tool calls, and state management that long-running agent sessions require.

This lands in the context of Nvidia's June announcement that Anthropic, OpenAI, and SpaceX are anchor customers for the chip — which, read alongside Anthropic's reported talks with Microsoft about Maia 200, the Anthropic IPO filing, and the Glasswing expansion, paints a picture of frontier labs spreading their hardware bets across Nvidia, in-house silicon, and hyperscaler ASICs simultaneously. The era when one company committed exclusively to one accelerator vendor is functionally over.

For learners: when the host CPU starts being designed around agent workloads rather than database queries, that's a signal about where the compute bottleneck has moved. Pay attention to which workloads each chip is being optimized for — agent orchestration, KV-cache management, long-context attention — because that road map tells you which AI capabilities will get cheap in the next 18 months.
